name: Layered Banking System — Exception Architecture
scenario: Build the exception handling architecture for a three-layer banking system (like HDFC's core banking backend). The system has a data layer (simulating JDBC), a service layer, and a presentation layer. Each layer uses the right exception type: data layer throws checked exceptions (external resource failures), service layer wraps into unchecked domain exceptions, presentation layer catches and returns messages. This is the architecture behind every professional Java backend.
description: Three-layer banking application demonstrating the complete checked-vs-unchecked exception pattern: checked IOException/DataException in the data layer, unchecked BankingException subclasses in the service layer, try-catch at the presentation boundary.

core.description:
Exception hierarchy:
- `BankingException extends RuntimeException` (unchecked base)
- `AccountNotFoundException extends BankingException` (account doesn't exist)
- `InsufficientFundsException extends BankingException` (not enough balance — include available and attempted amounts)
- `DataLayerException extends Exception` (checked — simulates JDBC/file failure)

`AccountRepository` (data layer): `Map<String, Double>` balances. `getBalance(String id)` throws checked `DataLayerException` when id starts with 'ERR'. `updateBalance(String id, double amount)` also throws DataLayerException for 'ERR' accounts.

`AccountService` (service layer): `transfer(from, to, amount)` — no throws declaration (unchecked). Validates then calls repository. Catches DataLayerException and rethrows as BankingException. Throws AccountNotFoundException or InsufficientFundsException for business rule failures.

`BankingPresentation` (presentation layer): `executeTransfer(from, to, amountStr)` — parses amount (NumberFormatException), calls service, catches all exceptions, returns user-friendly String messages.
Test 5 scenarios: successful transfer, insufficient funds, account not found, data layer failure (ERR prefix), invalid amount string.
Each exception carries relevant data: InsufficientFundsException has available and attempted fields; AccountNotFoundException has the missing account ID.

bonus.description: Add `TransactionLog` using `List<String>`. BankingPresentation appends every transfer attempt (success or failure) using finally.
Add `DailyLimitException extends BankingException` — throws if a single transfer exceeds ₹100,000.
Add `transferBatch(List<String[]> transfers)` — processes multiple transfers, continues on per-transfer failure (BankingException), stops on DataLayerException. Returns summary.

data_to_use:
Accounts:
- ACC-001: ₹50000
- ACC-002: ₹10000
- ACC-003: ₹5000

Test transfers:
1. ACC-001 → ACC-002 ₹15000 → success
2. ACC-003 → ACC-001 ₹10000 → InsufficientFundsException (has 5000)
3. ACC-999 → ACC-001 ₹1000 → AccountNotFoundException
4. ERR-001 → ACC-001 ₹1000 → DataLayerException wrapped in BankingException
5. ACC-001 → ACC-002 'abc' → NumberFormatException
example_output:
Transfer ACC-001→ACC-002 ₹15000: SUCCESS — ₹15000.0 transferred
Transfer ACC-003→ACC-001 ₹10000: FAILED — Insufficient funds: ACC-003 has ₹5000.0, needed ₹10000.0
Transfer ACC-999→ACC-001 ₹1000: FAILED — Account not found: ACC-999
Transfer ERR-001→ACC-001 ₹1000: FAILED — System error: Cannot access account ERR-001
Transfer ACC-001→ACC-002 abc: FAILED — Invalid amount: 'abc'

criteria: Service wraps checked in unchecked (10 pts)
Presentation catches all + parses amount (10 pts)
Meaningful messages with context data (5 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 hints. The key design choice: DataLayerException is checked because it simulates JDBC/file I/O failures — external. BankingException family is unchecked because they're domain rules — the service layer controls when they happen. The presentation layer is the right place to catch ALL exceptions and produce user-facing messages.
submission_instructions: Save as BankingSystem.java. Add a comment above each exception class explaining why it's checked or unchecked. Run all 5 scenarios. Push to GitHub — this is the architecture pattern used in every production Java banking system.
tips_for_success: DataLayerException extends Exception — the repository method signature includes `throws DataLayerException`.
Service layer: catch DataLayerException (checked), throw new BankingException('System error: '+cause.getMessage(), cause).
InsufficientFundsException constructor: (String accountId, double available, double attempted) — store all three fields.
Presentation layer: wrap the whole thing in try-catch, handle NumberFormatException for amount, BankingException for domain failures.
Use `e.getCause()` to show the original DataLayerException message in the BankingException.
concepts_reinforced: Checked exceptions for external resource failures
Unchecked exceptions for domain/business rule violations
Catching checked, rethrowing as unchecked (Spring pattern)
